rulings.Case Title: PIETRO PASQUALE-ANTONIO SGROMO VS. POLYGROUP INTERNATIONAL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Hearing For Reconsider Order Denying Moving Party'S Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER PIETRO SGROMO's Motion And Hearing For Reconsider Order Denying Moving Party'S Petition To Compel Arbitration Petitioner Pietro Pasquale Antonio Sgromo's motion for reconsideration of the Court's January 29, 2019 Order denying his amended petition to compel arbitration is denied. Petitioner has failed to show any new or different facts, circumstances or law than those before the Court at the time of the original ruling. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(b).) Petitioner's motion is premised on an unsigned Memorandum of Understanding that was before the Court on the first motion (Sgromo Decl., Exh. 15), and it does not support his position in any event. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Respondents is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JULIE LEE VS. STEVE LY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-17-562136 WITH CASE CGC-15-548732
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF JULIE LEE's MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-17-562136 WITH CASE CGC-15-548732 Plaintiff Julie Lee's motion to consolidate Case Nos. CGC-15-548732 and CGC-17-562136 is denied. Code of Civil Procedure section 1048 contemplates consolidation of pending actions either for pretrial purposes or for trial to enhance trial court efficiency and to avoid the danger of inconsistent adjudications. Here, the two actions involve distinct claims against different defendants-the first is no longer pending in that final judgment has already been entered, and the second has not yet proceeded to trial. Consolidation is not authorized or warranted under such circumstances. Plaintiff Julie Lee's motion for imposition of a creditor lien upon Defendant Zi Cheng, LLC is denied. No money judgment has been entered against Zi Cheng, LLC that could serve as the basis for such a lien. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LEANN M. RICHARDSON VS. FCA US LLC., A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion Motion For Attorneys Fees, Costs, And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF LEANN RICHARDSON MOTION - Motion For Attorneys Fees, Costs, And Expenses Plaintiff Leann M. Richardson's motion for an award of attorneys' fees is granted in the amount of $46,215. The Court finds the amount of time expended on the case and the monetary charge for that time to be excessive and unreasonable, particularly in light of the following factors: (i) the large number of attorneys (eleven, from two different law firms) who billed time on the case, which inevitably involved a great deal of duplication and inefficiency; (ii) the $43,000 in fees incurred by counsel in trial preparation between December 14, 2017, when Defendants made a section 998 offer in the amount of $120,000, and October 9, 2018, when the case settled at a mandatory settlement conference for the modestly higher amount of $127,500; (iii) the large amount of time billed for travel from Southern California to San Francisco, without any clear indication in the billing records as to whether any work on the case was actually performed during those hours; and (iv) the lack of novelty or complexity of the issues, particularly given counsel's subject matter expertise. For these reasons, in order to determine a reasonable award, the Court has reduced the amount of the lodestar by 50%, to $46,215. (See, e.g., Warren v. Kia Motors America, Inc. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 24, 37, 40-41 [trial court may apply a negative multiplier to adjust the lodestar figure to arrive at a reasonable fee if it "clearly explain[s] its case-specific reasons for the percentage reduction," which may properly include excessive time spent on case by multiple attorneys from different firms]; Save Our Uniquely Rural Community Environment v. County of San Bernardino (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1179, 1188 [trial court did not abuse its discretion in reducing lodestar by 80 percent].) The Court declines to award a positive multiplier. (See Hall v. FCA US LLC (E.D. Cal. May 21, 2018) 2018 WL 2298431 at *8 [declining to award multiplier to plaintiff's counsel in Song-Beverly Act case where, among other things, case did not present novel or difficult questions of law or fact, there was no evidence that the nature of the litigation precluded other employment by the attorneys, and the contingent nature of the fee award was outweighed by the other factors].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LEANN M. RICHARDSON VS. FCA US LLC., A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Plaintiffs Cost
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT MICHAEL STEAD'S AUTO DEPOT, INC.,, FCA US LLC's Motion To Tax Plaintiffs Cost Defendants FCA LLC and Michael Stead's Auto Depot, Inc.'s motion to tax plaintiff's costs is denied. As the prevailing party in this action under the Song-Beverly Act, plaintiff is entitled to recover "a sum equal to the aggregate amount of costs and expenses . . . determined by the court to have been reasonably incurred by the buyer in connection with the commencement and prosecution of such action." (Civ. Code § 1794(d).) Under the Song-Beverly Act, the Legislature intended the word "expenses" to cover items, such as expert fees, not included in the statutory definition of "costs" provided by Code of Civil Procedure section 1033.5. (Jensen v. BMW of North America (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 112, 137-38; see also Warren v. Kia Motors America, Inc. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 28, 42-43 [trial court erred in disallowing plaintiff's claim for trial transcripts].) The filing of a verified memorandum of costs is prima facie evidence that listed costs and expenses that appear facially proper were necessarily incurred. Defendants have not met their burden to show that any such costs or expenses were not reasonably or necessarily incurred. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: VAN LUONG VS. EMERALD WONG KAGEMOTO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answer To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Catherine Walsh In Support Thereof; [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT EMERALD WONG KAGEMOTO'S Motion To Compel Answer To Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Conor Moore, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's unopposed motion to compel responses to form interrogatories, requests for production of documents, and special interrogatories from Plaintiff Van Luong is Granted. Plaintiff shall produce code-compliant responses to Defendant's form interrogatories, requests for production of documents, and special interrogatories no later than March 21, 2019. Defendant's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sfjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KRISTY HARRIS VS. ON DEMAND PROFESSIONAL SECURITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Approving Paga Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFF KRISTY HARRIS' Motion For An Order Approving Paga Settlement Plaintiff Kristy Harris's motion for an order approving PAGA settlement is granted. No opposition filed, and the settlement meets the standards for approval under Lab. Code § 2699. In particular, the parties have not agreed to release or discharge any PAGA claims by any other individual, whether brought individually or on behalf of other aggrieved employees. The Court finds that the allocation of $15,000 in civil penalties to the settlement of plaintiff's PAGA claims, in relation to the settlement amount allocated to plaintiff's individual claims in the parties' confidential settlement agreement (which the Court has reviewed in camera), is reasonable in light of plaintiff's statement of damages and the maximum civil penalties, wages, damages, and attorneys' fees that could be awarded on those respective claims, defendants' potential defenses to the PAGA claims, and the concerns regarding the collectability of a judgment against defendants, among other factors. The Court finds that the proposed agreement is fair and adequate in view of the purposes and policies of the statute. (See Flores v. Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, Inc. (C.D. Cal. 2017) 253 F.Supp.3d 1074, 1077, citing O'Connor v. Uber Techs., Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2016) 201 F.Supp.3d 1110, 1132-35.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: K NIELSEN VS. TEVA SALES AND MARKETING INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANTs TEVA SALES AND MARKETING INC, and EYAD MATAR's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendants Teva Sales and Marketing, Inc. and Eyad Matar's motion to strike portions of plaintiff K. Nielsen's complaint is denied. Under Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, a plaintiff can seek punitive damages if an employer fraudulently induces her to enter into an employment contract because by "pursuing a valid fraud action, a plaintiff advances the public interest in punishing intentional misrepresentations and in deterring such misrepresentations in the future." (Id. at 639.) Unlike Brewer v. Premier Golf Properties, LP (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1243, 1250, the complaint does not seek punitive damages based solely on a Labor Code violation; rather, the punitive damages are sought for the alleged fraudulent inducement to accept defendants' offer of employment by the use of false promises. Further, plaintiff is allowed to plead alternative theories of recovery. (See Cortez v. Purolator Air Filtration Products Co. (2000) 23 Cal.4th 163, 174 ["unpaid wages might be recovered as damages in a civil suit for breach of contract or one premised on fraud or misrepresentation theories. It does not follow, however, that when the failure to pay wages violates the Labor Code and therefore constitutes an unfair business practice the wages owed may not be recovered as restitution in a UCL action."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: K NIELSEN VS. TEVA SALES AND MARKETING INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 11, (tentative ruling 1 of 2) DEFENDANTs TEVA SALES AND MARKETING INC, EYAD MATAR's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants Teva Sales and Marketing, Inc. and Eyad Matar's demurrer to the first through fifth causes of action of plaintiff K. Nielsen's complaint is sustained with 20 days' leave to amend. Ms. Nielsen has not pled sufficient facts to support her claim for gender discrimination in violation of FEHA. Plaintiff has exaggerated the allegations in her complaint, which nowhere alleges that she was "subjected to a litany of sexist slurs and comments by her supervisor" or that he asserted that "sexual harassment has been a fixture of the workplace, and that there's no need to change it." (Opp. at 7, 9.) At most, the complaint alleges that Ms. Nielsen objected to and disagreed with a male supervisor's sexist remarks regarding women who claimed to have been subjected to sexual harassment and criticizing the "Me Too" movement. (Compl. 10, 12, 14-19.) She has not pled facts, however, showing that her gender was a motivating factor for her termination, as opposed to a personal disagreement she had with her supervisor. Ms. Nielsen is granted leave to amend this claim, if she can do so in good faith. The complaint fails to allege sufficient facts to state a claim for harassment on the basis of sex on a hostile work environment theory. "[T]o establish liability in FEHA hostile work environment sexual harassment case, a plaintiff employee must show she was subjected to sexual advances, conduct, or comments that were severe enough or sufficiently pervasive to alter the conditions of her employment and create a hostile or abusive work environment." (Lyle v. Warner Brothers Television Productions (2006) 38 Cal.4th 264, 283; accord, Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1043 ("the hostile work environment form of sexual harassment is actionable only when the harassing behavior is pervasive or severe."].) Under this standard, "an employee generally cannot recover for harassment that is occasional, isolated, sporadic, or trivial; rather, the employee must show a concerted pattern of harassment of a repeated, routine, or a generalized nature." (Id.) Thus, "an employee seeking to prove sexual harassment based on no more than a few isolated incidents of harassing conduct must show that the conduct was 'severe in the extreme.'" (Hughes v. Pair, 46 Cal.4th at 1043.) While evidence of hostile, sexist statements is relevant to show discrimination on the basis of sex, "it is not necessarily sufficient, by itself, to establish actionable conduct." (Lyle, 38 Cal.4th at 281.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: K NIELSEN VS. TEVA SALES AND MARKETING INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Teva Sales And Marketing Inc, Eyad Matar Demurrer To Complaint (Tentative Ruling 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 11, Defendant Teva Sales And Marketing Inc, Eyad Matar Demurrer To Complaint (Tentative Ruling 2 Of 2) Here, plaintiff's allegations regarding two conversations she had with her supervisor over the course of six months of employment, between September 2017 and March 2018, during which he expressed sexist sentiments, do not remotely establish that the complained-of conduct was either pervasive or severe. (See, e.g., Hughes v. Pair, 46 Cal.4th at 1048-49 [plaintiff's factual allegations were not sufficient to establish a claim where the alleged sexual harassment was neither pervasive, consisting only of comments defendant made to plaintiff during a single telephone conversation and a brief statement defendant made to plaintiff in person later that day, nor severe]; Brennan v. Townsend & O'Leary Enterprises, Inc. (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 1336, 1353-59 [insufficient evidence supported jury's finding plaintiff was subjected to severe or pervasive harassment based on her gender where, among other things, she inadvertently received a single derogatory email, witnessed only three incidents of gender-based conduct involving coworkers over the span of several years, and her manager initiated conversations with her about her personal life].) In view of plaintiff's vague allegation that for "several weeks" after the first interaction, Mr. Matar made similar remarks about the physical appearance of other Bay Area sales representatives (Compl. 15), however, she is granted leave to amend this cause of action if she can do so in good faith. The complaint also fails to allege sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action for retaliation. Plaintiff again mischaracterizes her own allegations in the complaint, asserting that she made "multiple complaints to Matar objecting to his sexist comments" (Opp. at 15) and that defendants made a "decision not to take any action to investigate [her] complaints about sexual harassment." (Opp.at 8.) Nowhere in the complaint, however, does she allege that she ever made any such complaint, whether to Mr. Matar or to any other representative of Teva, regarding any unlawful employment practice. Plaintiff's attempt to recharacterize her social media post as "clearly referencing Matar's comments that day in December" (Compl. 15) is unconvincing, as that post, which the complaint quotes at length (Compl. 16), made no reference to Mr. Matar, and instead referenced online sexual harassment to which plaintiff had been subjected as a result of her "coverage of the federal government shutdown." The demurrer to the retaliation cause of action is sustained with leave to amend. The demurrers as to the causes of action for failure to prevent a violation of FEHA and for wrongful termination are also sustained with leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DOCSEND, INC. VS. INSIDESALES.COM, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To File Under Seal Complaint And Exhibit A
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, March 11, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF DOCSEND, INC.'s Motion To File Under Seal Complaint And Exhibit A Off Calendar per request of the moving party (email dated 3/8/19). =(302/EPS)
